title: Breaking a broody hen
subtitle: Ending a broody cycle in a laying hen using the cool-airflow method, without eggs or a cockerel.
excerpt: A broody hen sits tight on the nest, stops laying, loses condition, and can hold the broody state for three weeks or longer if undisturbed. Breaking the cycle early, within two to three days of it starting, gets the hen back into lay within a week. The cool-airflow method works for most breeds.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Laying Hens (2002). https://www.gov.uk

content.text: when rising prolactin levels trigger the urge to incubate a clutch. It can happen without a cockerel and without fertile eggs; the trigger is daylength, a suitable nest, and a warm underside when the hen settles. The same hormone that drives the broody state also suppresses ovulation, so a broody hen stops laying for the duration. In a laying flock without cockerels, this is pure loss.

attrs.body: A broody hen sits on the nest box for most of the day and night. When lifted off she puffs up, clucks loudly, and may peck at your hand. She is hotter than the other birds: the brood patch on her belly will be visibly featherless and warm to the touch. She will lose weight over a long broody spell because she leaves the nest rarely to feed and drink.
attrs.title: Identifying a broody hen

content.text: The broody state is maintained by heat under the

. Cool airflow disrupts the temperature signal and the hormone cycle breaks down within two to three days. Put the hen in a wire-floored anti-broody crate, elevated off the ground on blocks or hung from a hook so air circulates under the floor. Provide food and water inside the crate. The wire floor means the hen cannot get the insulating ground contact that reinforces the brood patch warmth.

Most hens break within three days in a crate. Check each morning by lifting the hen: if she is still fluffed and clucking, return her to the crate. If she is calm, put her back with the flock and watch the nest box. A hen that returns straight to the nest is not broken. Most will begin laying again within seven to ten days of breaking.

Dunking a broody hen in cold water is an older remedy that sometimes works but causes unnecessary stress and, in cold weather, the risk of chill. Do not use it. Removal from the flock without the crate, simply shutting her out of the coop during the day, rarely breaks a determined broody and wastes the time it takes to establish.
